text:
"So, the way I see it, Tashi loves neither Art nor Patrick. She loves to live vicariously through Art and share in his success (as signified by her changing the poster from 'game changer' to 'game changers') since she can't play Tennis anymore herself. His success is her success, hence her screaming "we won" when Art defeated Patrick in the end. But I think Art actually loves her.

Tashi feels intense passion for Patrick as he actually challenges her and doesn't just do anything she says like Art does. That's why she keeps sleeping with him. But she doesn't love him, either.

As for Art and Patrick hugging at the end, I think they both just really enjoyed playing Tennis with each other again, something they bonded over in the past. Tennis was the core of their friendship. And now they come together again with Art being really successful, unlike Patrick, who couldn't turn Tennis into an actual career. So, with the tables being turned (as Patrick was better in the past), they have a really tight match. They're on a more equal footing than they were in the past, so they have an exciting match, which is all Patrick wanted, as he told Art in the Sauna.""
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"My interpretation was that competition (in tennis or relationships) can be either a destructive force or help you be your best self.

Patrick is the Challenger in Art's tennis and marriage. Without him around their marriage has become a stale business arrangement and Art doesn't have the love of the game he needs to win. Without Art Patrick doesn't have anyone who motivates him to work hard.

As for Tashi I think she does love Art but she's more attracted to Patrick and she needs both those things to realize her ambitions. Tashi and Patrick together are passionate to the point of recklessness and Tashi and Art are more compatible but also kind of a marriage of convenience. When they come together again in the end those qualities are balanced out and they can all succeed."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"I think the ending represents a moment of grace she extends to her father. That final glance represents how father and daughter finally see each other clearly, maybe for the first time. I like to think it's a first step towards some form of healing, but not a full reconciliation (note that they don't actually speak to each other (or maybe even know what to do with each other) after the scene wraps). Warmth only comes when the grandson runs in to greet them, the grandson possibly representing how Agnes finally breaks the cycle of trauma. It's quite interesting that Gustav is actually pretty good with his grandson (except for the DVDs he gave him lol)! Although again, most, if not all his interactions with his grandson centre around art.

Or maybe Gustav disappears again, like what he did with young Agnes after he made his movie with her. But regardless of what happens next, father and daughter have this moment together"
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Yeah, the ending I think it explicitly showing her opening to her having a relationship with her father, pretty much solely due to the screenplay presenting a vulnerability and attempt to understand her that clearly she's never felt before.

I think it's also worth considering what happens with the house at the end of the film, because essentially the family home is character unto itself, and since its fate basically bookends the film, it's important to the story.

Gustav intrudes into the family home despite not living there for decades, and presumes it to be the set where he'll shoot his film. He conducts rehearsals with Rachel Kemp there, yet the whole thing is kind of a poor facsimile for the emotional truth or reality of his mother, perhaps best (and most humorously) represented by the stool he casually tells Rachel his mother used to kill herself, despite it being an Ikea stool. The house, like the casting of Rachel herself, is Gustav straining to connect with the two most important women in his life, his mother and his daughter, and yet he doesn't really understand both just yet."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"I think it's helpful to see The Conversation and its ending in terms of the limits of information. We have a character, Harry Caul, who seemingly has a god-like omniscience in terms of what he can hear - he has access to intimate, seemingly private conversations. He has access to all information, yet he misreads it. Though Caul's omniscience is in terms of hearing, our metaphors tend towards sight, so I say that though Caul appears to be all seeing, there are extreme limits to that sight, which eventually destroy him.

Caul's catholicism is treated very respectfully and it's an essential part for him - when he destroys a ceramic religious figure in order to find the bug, it's a self-destruction. He looks at the women in catholic terms, and this is a limit in his vision: there is the bad girl (Amy) who has sex in exchange for rent money; there is the good girl (Ann) who is in trouble and he must save. His vision, however, is entirely wrong - Amy truely cares for him, and Ann is using him as a patsy.

To focus on the bug at the end as something physical, as something concrete to be located, is a misunderstanding of the movie, I think. Caul is all-seeing, his expertise should allow him to locate any bug, yet he cannot. We have a last, metaphorical expression of someone all seeing, all hearing, who cannot locate the underlying thing of essential importance."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I'd take Coppola's explanation of the ending as the only concrete theory -that it could be taken multiple ways. Either Caul was delusional, or the bug was hidden in the saxophone (this has always been my reading of the ending as well). On that note, the idea that the ending is uncertain is a very meaningful bookend to the whole point of the film. This movie came out only two years after the Watergate scandal, and the rapidly-evolving surveillance state/culture in America was yet mostly uncharted territory for social theorists, which made it perfect fodder for a director like Coppola. Based on how much cocaine the man was doing during the making of Apocalypse Now, I'd guess his main point in The Conversation has to do with the relationship between the state of surveillance technology and the political economy and systemic paranoia among the general public. The fact that the leading authority on surveillance tech (Caul) falls victim to such a deep state of paranoia that he destroys his entire apartment really drives that point home, and the mist of uncertainty that surrounds the ending gives the viewer a very similar feeling of unease."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The ending is purposefully ambiguous. We're meant to be thinking "is she happy he's there", "is she now with someone else and feels awkward that she has to tell Sutter", "has she realised how damaging he was and doesn't want him there", and more. In the end I don't think it matters what exactly her expression is saying.

Before that final shot the film could already end, we know that Sutter now has all he needs to make himself a better person. His teacher misses the chance to convince Sutter of why education is important, his boss misses the chance to make Sutter confront his alcoholism, but then his Mum doesn't miss the chance to tell him he's a person of worth and that he's not just his father. Him going to see Aimee lets us know that he is now thinking of the future and hopefully that means he'll start to think about education and how his damaging alcoholism is. We can't be certain that will happen but we at least know he's on the right path."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The whole movie romanticizes Sutter’s obsession with living in the now, and the ending almost feels like a form of torture — not just for him, but for us too. It leaves you suspended in that same nostalgic limbo he’s trapped in. There’s no neat resolution, no “happily ever after,” and that absence of closure feels deliberate, like the film is forcing us to sit in that uncertainty with him.

For me, Aimee’s look isn’t rejection. It’s more like she’s silently asking him, “Are you actually ready?” Sutter’s young, and his life isn’t ruined, but the protective bubble he’s been living in is about to burst. His *“It’s not too late for me”*moment is tender and hopeful, but the film doesn’t give us the comfort of seeing what comes next — because in real life, realizations don’t automatically fix everything.

What makes it stick with me is that Sutter feels almost like a memory or a dream. Without a clear ending, you’re not ready to let him go. If we saw him happy or at peace, we could move on. But we don’t. It’s like how Cassidy asks him not to visit her — sometimes holding on to the past stops you from moving forward. The film leaves us in that exact emotional space, wondering what happens after the now."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
" don't think it was one of those "the one that got away" type love stories. The outcome is realistic. You love someone but you need to go away to college or move for a job, life gets in the way, and long distance relationships become hard to keep up. New people enter your life and you find yourself moving on. Real life is often not about some passionate love that transcends all circumstances. A lot of relationships work and don't work because of timing. And I think we underestimate how simple pragmatism effects the survival of these relationships.

I think both of them wonder what could have been if things were different, or had they met at a different point in their lives, but I don't think they regret how their lives turned out or the choices they made. But they also certainly don't regret meeting each other and the love affair they had. So I say it's not a lie. It's very much the truth of how relationships pan out."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The primary message of the story is to emphasize how important it is to have positive traits like honesty, humility and kindness represented in Charlie. This is opposed to the children who represent greed, pride, and selfishness. The story shows a dark sense of humor about karmic retribution shown to each child.

I think one thing to keep in mind is that the source material and author Roald Dahl had a pretty dark sense of humor for a children’s writer. His worlds often shared similar themes and slightly askew perspectives of reality with more cartoonish interpretations of good vs evil and children often overcoming abusive adults."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"In that line, Wonka could be talking about himself - “the man who suddenly got everything he ever wanted” would be Wonka, who just found the right person to take over his life’s work. He will live happily ever after. This is the great gift that Charlie has given to Wonka. Or it could be Wonka’s warped fractured fairytale persona at work. Either way, it’s not the theme of the movie.

Just earlier, when Charlie puts the everlasting gobstopper down on his desk, Wonka clutches it and says, “so shines a good deed in a weary world.” Charlie’s act of selflessness is the pivotal action of the movie and Wonka’s line is pretty much the Big Theme."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"It’s very straight-forward - it’s a feel-good ending. Charlie is an imperfect but ultimately good and honest child, and is rewarded for that. The line is set up to subvert expectations, fitting with Wonka’s personality and reinforcing it as a “happily ever after” fairy tale sort of ending. But aside from that:

Why is it that “You’re not supposed to get everything you ever wanted”? Obviously most people don’t, but why is it inherently bad for people to achieve their dreams? Why are you taking it as an inherent truth that someone who gets what they want “is probably not a happy man because he wants more”? In that case, who is happy - you’re essentially saying both that everyone wants more and that anyone who wants more isn’t happy.

I think I see where you’re coming from - something like “Kids shouldn’t think their happiness is tied in to material possessions”. But to me, it seems unrealistic and frankly silly to try to completely remove any element of “want” from children’s messaging."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: " I think that the real driving force behind Mommy is this absolute love for transient, temporal happiness. Before the three characters exist in their world together, nobody's happy--Steve is violent, Di is stressed, and Kyla is a homebody grieving a personal loss. But the connection that they share--however short and doomed--is one that opens up their worlds and lets a little bit of light in for the very first time in a very long time."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"As said by other users, an easy parallel can be made with Crimes and Misdemeanors (one of my favorite films ever): Allen's idea is that there is no destiny or force that drives men to repentance, in reality the protagonist idealist loses completely and the killer is never discovered and over time manages to suppress his repentance. At some point the two protagonists (the idealist and the murderer) meet and the first says that according to him there must be a punishment for someone who commits a crime, be it repentance, death or capture, but the killer with a smile points out that they are not in a movie: Allen's message as always is that cinema is a better world than the real one in which the good ones win, the bad ones lose and the narrative arcs are completed, when in reality this never happens.

Match Point is very nice, but I think it's more about how a man in order to get rich and maintain a rich social status is willing to do anything and that people will always be willing to believe him. because a rich intellectual)."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"This movie is an exploration of the idea of Loyalty. Why is a man loyal to his company? Why does a sportsman stick to his agent rather that go in for the best available deal? Why does Rod's wife expect his brother Tee Pee to not trash talk about him? ("Show some loyalty" she says). And why does a man stick to his woman? Why does he go back to her every time, and only her, when he is besieged by options, riddled with confusions, bogged down by concerns that seem far more important than romantic love?

Jerry's answer to that question was that he is just the guy who sticks, as if he is somehow morally constrained to do so. Until he sees in the greatest moment of Rod's and perhaps his own life ("a very very big night"), that what made it complete for Rod was the act of sharing it with his family. In that moment of reflection he realises that it wasn't a compulsion to be the guy that sticks, sticking around was in fact the very key to his happiness.

In other words, the switch wasn't sudden at all. He has been wrestling with the notion of loyalty through the movie (and perhaps way before), until in that moment of clarity, he finally gets it. That loyalty is not what you owe someone. Its what makes you happy.""
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The eggs I think are similar to when Oliver is hesitant to start a relationship with Elio because once he starts he would also over-indulge. I also noticed lots of doorways -- shots moving through them, even a scene where a door is loudly banging open and closed in the wind--probably symbolic of coming out of the closet or entering a new place. The major thing I didn't grasp was the relevance of Judaism to the story. It plays a significant role but I didn't figure out the main point of that."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The apricot-metaphor is pretty clear. Some things I would add: the etymological discussion about the word 'apricot' lets us know that the word derives from a word that means 'pre-ripe'. To me, this enhances the metaphor of forbidden fruit and Elios "pre-ripe" state. Also, during their getaway, Elio throws up while they are out at night, and his throw-up looks like yellow apricot mush. This to me shows that he is drunk on love and sexuality.

I hadn't thought about the eggs, but the wound on Olivers side seems to represent something as well, as it is shown repeatedly in different stages of healing. Maybe it symbolises his shame towards his homosexuality? Dunno."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "One thing that the movie doesn’t include from the book is that during the scene when Elio gets a nosebleed Oliver actually started stroking Elio’s feet with his own under the table, and that’s what causes Elio to get the nosebleed. I don’t think there is any kind of specific symbolism with feet, i think it goes along with the general appreciation of naked bodies, and that lots of characters walk around without shirts or shoes adds to the languid mood of the movie, it’s just more sensual. In the book Elio devotes a lot of time to thinking about and describing Oliver’s body, and he does mention his feet a lot as well."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Nostalghia was Tarkovsky trying his hardest to win the Palm d'Or at Cannes, so he may had been indeed indulging himself a little too much with those kind of symbolic images (off the top of my head I remember one at the beginning with a bunch of white pigeons coming out of a Virgin Mary statue that felt, at least for me at the time, quite forced and pretentious), but I do think he was onto something true in his book. You won't evolve as an artist if you always stick to whatever rules you adhere yourself to."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Warawara are complex 4D hieroglyphs- from hieros, "sacred," and glyphein, "to carve." See Tsimshian Soul Catchers and Raven Rattles for mind-boggling 3-D hieroglyphs.

The film deploys esoteric symbolism to hint that the secret to creating human souls resembles angels excreting in reverse: we witness white (death) liquid souls come up out of the ground (of being) and then float up into the sky to become food (for the "gods"). There is nothing else for the gods to eat, so the digested souls then fall to the ground like rain to be reabsorbed and resecreted. (Only have of this sequence is expicitly animated in the film)."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "n the film's final act, immediately after Howard is beaten up and thrown in the fountain, the scene cuts to him returning to work and for the remainder of the film the colors are washed out in white. We usually think of this symbolizing purity and innocence but in Jewish culture, which is obviously central to the story, it also signifies death. And in contrast to all three of these, we have black, the color of the opal. After a cursory reading of the symbolic significance in Judaism, I found that black is the origin of all other colors, as the universe was black in the beginning but gave birth the all light upon God speaking it into existence (or however people think that worked). This makes sense as we enter the story through the refraction of light in the opal, then soar through its nebular inner workings, and emerge through Howard"s asshole and into 2012 Manhattan. And we exit the film through the opal and out into the wider universe of stars."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I thought it was cool how the same object was utilised so differently by the different classes. The surface-level point of the rock is that it is supposed to be a decorative, good-luck charm. Beyond that, it is not intended to have any practicality or use, it isn't a commodity and is designed to be purchased by people who can afford to buy something with no purpose or use. Throughout the film you get the consistent idea of just how much more resourceful the poorer characters are - whether that's through the Photoshop scene at the beginning or the more in-your-face aspect of the family creating this meticulous plan to take up employment under the same family. They find a way to live while at subsistence levels because they have to. Since the rock is used at the end, in a poor character's hands, to very atavistically bash someone's head in, I guess it's supposed to suggest that even the most useless, purposeless objects will be given a use in the hands of poorer people. Even in futile situations with seemingly nothing at hand, the resourcefulness that poverty installs in you will always give you an out."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
" interpreted it as symbolising a 'get rich quick scheme' and material greed. Min says it will grant their family material wealth, and afterwards is when they start 'scheming' and looking for a shortcut, whereas before they had legitimate endeavours — small businesses and contracts like folding the pizza boxes. Ki-woo says "it's so metaphorical" at first when he's staring in amazement at it, but he doesn't really know what it represents or why, he's just projecting his hope about Min's claims.

At the end, when Ki-woo is having his future/dream sequence, he places and discards of the rock in the creek, camouflaging the rock amongst the others. He no longer wants to go for a 'get rich quick scheme', and outlines his plan for the future to work legitimately and save up for years."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "However, the original name of the ending OST, '564 years' indicates the amount of time someone in his salary range would have to realistically work to be able to buy that mansion on top of the hill, and be able to 'cross the line' from the lower to upper class — since the late stage capitalism in South Korea is so bad that there's essentially no middle class."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The rock also turned out to float — Ki-woo misinterpreted this as him being destined for wealth, as all people obsessed with the capitalist dream are, but in reality the rock is fake and hollow. This also symbolises how the upper class can make easy profits and huge returns due to the monopolisation of resources; some parking spaces can earn more per hour than most average wages around the world (read that in another thread). And also selling the false, capitalist dream that they can achieve that status and wealth too through hard work and determination alone — though in 'late' stage capitalism it's more due to privilege and luck."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The feather at the very beginning and the very ending of Forrest Gump. I still haven't found a proper symbolism for that.

The girl in the red coat in Schindler's List. That girl is the only thing in the whole movie (save for the first and last scene) that isn't in black-and-white. My interpretation is that it symbolizes innocence of childhood, and that that innocence is the only thing that isn't grey in this ugly world, or something like that.

Everything in Willy Wonka's office being in half in Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory. I read somewhere that the director wanted to make something stand out about that scene, or otherwise it would seem like a generic office scene. Bit my interpretation is that Willy Wonka devoted half of his life to creating this chocolate factory, and now this other half is all he has left. Or something like that."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The guy in the mask going down on another guy in The Shining. There's no explanation for it in the movie but here's the explanation if you read the book: "In the book, the man dressed in a suit is actually Horace Derwent, the mysterious owner of the Overlook Hotel, and the man in the costume (which is a dog in the book) is Roger, a one-time lover of his. The novel provides Roger and Horace's story as an example of atrocities that have happened in the hotel""
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The color schemes in Hero. That movie was loaded with colors representing the meaning behind each telling of the hero's story. Red for the lie, blue for the emperors guess at the truth, white for the real story, and so on"
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"I got the impression that Candie in a sense represented the South right before the Civil War. He dresses well, he combs his hair, he's very neat, but his eyes look sunken in and colorless and his teeth are rotten. He's pretty on the outside,but he's decaying, like an old plantation house. He's a Francophile and he makes people call him Monsieur, but he doesn't speak French and he would NEVER let Schutz know that he has no fucking idea what "Prosit!" means. German, I know, but the same idea.

He's a beautiful symbol of a society that is, despite its false air of sophistication, out of touch with the world around it and, for all its extravagance, in decay, both morally and socially. For both that reason and the fact he's a great villain I consider him the best character that Tarantino has ever written, by far."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "We had just seen Candie practically win the game of wits and such at the Dinner table. He acts as Dr. King puts it “a sore winner”. He then brings out the cake. He’s literally trying to have his cake and eat it too. And the fact the film revolves around slavery and racism the specificity of the cake being white cake adds to this gesture."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Django is alchemy, magnum opus. the lead becomes gold. the spirit is perfected.
there's four stages for the great work: nigredo(black, lead, spiritual darkness), albedo(white, separation of the principles, purification), citrinitas(yellow, sun, anouncing the success of the great work), rubedo(red, gold, reunification of the principles)

Django is found in the night by King Schultz - judge, mayor, who carries a light - Lucifer < lux+fero, light bearer.
Old man from Carrucan in green clothes - green lion, against a bright white background separates Django from Hildi, and says "boy got sand" - he is no good. Then they go to Big Daddy and its' a white mannor with two sets of everything.
Then Django has visions of Hildi in a yellow dress, in a yellow field, we meet her at CC(calvin candy) ranch in a yellow light.
Then lots of blood. Django lights a candle and does exactly same killer move as King Schultz when they met.

The work is complete, Hildi and Django reunite, Django is dressed in gold and purple - colors of the emperor."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Charlie cared for the bird because he has an innate desire to care for others more than himself. Ellie shatters the plate when she sees her father taking care of a bird, despite being absent for most of her life. She's jealous/angry that he'll care for a bird but not for her."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I think the bird may be Alan's spirit/metaphor since Charlie takes care of it by feeding it just like how he did for Alan when he was alive. However, we never see the apples in the plate fully gone which may represent Alan's eating disorder, not finishing his food. When Ellie breaks the plate, this may be due to jealousy towards Alan for being taken cared of by Charlie who failed to take care of his own daughter. By the end, the window sill is filled with a bright light which may represent Alan being in the afterlife and how Charlie will eventually be faced with that light and see Alan again."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "it represent life and the beauty Charlie sees in the world, but mostly his soul wanting to escape out of the body that traps him. The plate where he feeds the bird could be hope (to get closure with his daughter) when it breaks it’s a changing point in the story where despair and death creep in."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I felt that the bird was less symbolic and more a tool. In one case, the bird is used to question Ellie’s state of mind and her potential for evil. She has so much anger and rage in her for the abandonment she felt by her father. She was obsessed with death and even posted photos of dead animals on her social media page. Not knowing whether or not the bird was killed/murdered by Ellie questions the future of Ellie—is she really evil or is there hope for her? Did she really send the photos and recording to the parents of the missionary to help him, or was it to destroy him?"
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Charlie always chose to see the good in the world around him—just as his ex-wife stated. He refused to believe that his daughter could be anything other than misunderstood. Charlie could not except reality—one in which his daughter had a lot of potential for evil. He had to believe that there was good in her."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I think this film is a good portion dedicated to the nature over nurture theory. Does one become evil or is one born evil? In Ellie’s case, she wasn’t necessarily born evil, but became evil based off of her environment. She was obviously born good because of her compassion toward the whale in the novel Moby Dick. It’s what kept Charlie hanging on, believing that his daughter could see deeper into the novel, one in which the world interprets as plainly as a boat captain’s hunt for the greatest prize. In Ellie’s interpretation it was about Melville’s internal struggles and she captured this thru the boring/fluff chapters. This is extremely insightful and leads one to believe that Ellie has insight and empathy."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "She broke the plate but did not hurt the bird. There was likely some jealousy but I believe she wanted the bird to go home to his family. As she wish her father had done and what the missionary kid did."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The missionary was the bird. I noticed he only ever shows up after the bird. Bird, then a shadow passes the window, then knock knock.. The shadow is there between the bird and the arrival of the missionary every. time. bird - shadow - knock. It's quick, but it's there."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "For me I kept thinking, the reason you have bird feeders is to attract birds and keep them coming back. So there is a connection between leaving food for the bird and offering Ellie the money/writing her essays, and even Liz who is emotionally connected to him bc of her brother and the false pretense of “poor Charlie”. He was doing what he could to keep them all coming back and the shattered plate signaled the unraveling of all that. The fact that Ellie did it was part of her “monster kid” storyline because she wanted him alone and miserable (including trying to get rid of the missionary)."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "the way i saw it was that the parks reduced a whole culture of people to an accessory, which is something that privileged people do often in society. Its the indifference and sort of entitlement that ties in. u/Mamlucky brought up another good point and potential way it ties in: "The major difference was that the Park father could put on a cheap costume portraying an oppressed people and play make believe with his son for an afternoon; Kim was legitimately oppressed and there was no escape. Park's revulsion to the odor of a poor man was perfect on so many levels, but I feel his Native American theme party was icing on the cake. He can play "American Indian" with his family by getting a fake teepee, walkie talkies, and camp site, but when faced with the reality of class inequality, his fragile sensibilities can't take the heat.""
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Ultimately, the feather in Forrest Gump touches on these themes symbolically. The feather first shows up during the film's opening, floating wistfully until it falls at Forrest's feet. He puts it into his suitcase, before beginning to tell the story of his life. The symbol doesn't come back into play until the film's very end, after he's sent his son on the bus for his first day of school. The feather represents Forrest's approach to life — it just floats through the world in whichever direction the wind takes it. A lot of what happens is up to chance — for good and for bad."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The feather flying adrift for me means that we don't need to force anything or demand anything from God, because if we do the best with what God gives us we will obtain peace and success, we go where God sends us, just like the feather, and we have to take advantage of the opportunities that are in our path."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Further to other comments, the white feather has also been used to symbolise cowardice and pacifism in many cultures. However, in the US it has been given to soldiers to symbolise instead bravery.

To tie to Forrest, he serves as a soldier in Vietnam. He is also brave in that war. During the course of the film, Forrest reflects right wing values more than left wing ones. Meanwhile Jenny more follows left wing values. They both go with the tide of the times but Forrest follows duty to achieve a freedom, Jenny follows personal liberty to achieve a freedom. They both seem to float like feathers on the wind but Forrest has a secret core, a railroad track of all American goodness that can be mistaken by others as lacking 'gump'tion."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The entire film is about a man who doesn't give up even when others think he is foolish or treat him badly. He sometimes unconsciously struggles against the social fashions of the time. At the same time, he's of that time in the 60s and 70s when seeking new adventure was the thing. The feather gets blown around, it can look delicate, but it retains its shape, like Forrest."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"I think that's the point of the movie that is kind of missed the most. The Colonel was full of crap, got one of his men killed, and threw everybody else under the bus, all for his vanity.

Everything him and the Lt played by Kiefer Sutherland talked or boasted about was posturing, and they shat on the values they were meant to uphold.

There are, indeed, bad guys in the world, and there is a need for a few good men to stand "on the wall", but in any armed forces, there are psychos like them who thrive by being full of crap. And the more prevalent they are, the more full of crap these armed forces are as a whole."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Furthermore it’s that Jessup represents an old way and the defence attorneys represent a new way—you have the pansy pretty boy, the woman, the Jewish intellectual who at one point identifies with Santiago as the weak kid subject to bullying. Those 3 don’t belong in the military that Jessup served and rose up through the ranks of.

Jessup thinks in an old-school mentality way like he’s in WWII, Korea, Vietnam—that he has every right to order Santiago, Dawson and Downy to all charge up a hill to their certain deaths if it means a slight improvement to his strategic position. And his judgment of that strategic move would be beyond question.

So he ought to have just as much right to bury them in a legal battle in order to protect his own judgment from being questioned by the likes of those attorneys."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The sense you pick up along the way is kind of like Platoon where the guys are split between Elias and Barnes. Jessup is a political figure, and he has people below him that make decisions on political merit, to cover his ass and shield him from the emerging changes to the military and its code. Jessup is no longer a superior who is genuinely trying to take the hill and regrettably forced to sacrifice some of his men to that task. He is now a man whose task is to maintain the code that he believes makes the military great—(because he ego-identifies with it)—and he’s willing to make legal, political, vocational sacrifices of his men to win. And his hold on power is essential to that task and so it is beyond question that it is right for him to do those things."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"One of the biggest themes in the movie is that we are told about how things are, and are lead to believe them without actually seeing them as they are.

The Wizard, being a pretty foremost example, as his “wonderful ness” comes merely from the “wonderful things he does” and the Munchkin’s blindly sing about him, and so does Dorothy and her friends. They constantly sing about this man whom they’ve never seen, nor really know the deeds of beyond said deeds being “wonderful.”

No one really ever talks about how the lyrics seem purposely parodic, as the Munchkins seems desperate to come up with a reason to make the Wizard seem great, sort of stuttering: “Because, because, because, because...” and with good reason, “because” they don’t have any actual reason to believe the Wizard is great, they just fall for propaganda that he is."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"Another thing about the Munchkin’s that is fascinating, in my opinion, is their absolute hatred of the Wicked Witch of the East. Like, we never really get a good sense of what said Witch was actually did to deserve such a bombastically over the top celebration of her death. Which, if compared to the Wizard, makes you wonder if the Witch of the East was actually “evil” at all.

It all ties back to the main theme, with the Scarecrow, Tinman, and Lion. They each present you with the idea that the lack certain qualities, and you take that at face value. But, over the course of the movie, you learn that they were wrong, and of course they have those things! Even Dorothy, who wanted to go home, had said ability all along.

It’s an interesting observation because, the movie presents itself in a very aesthetically interesting way, but, the movie also, perhaps purposefully, uses its aesthetic to distract us from the idea that it’s not really showing us anything, and it’s merely telling us what to believe."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I felt that the ending represented the idea that sometimes if you tar someone with a particular brush enough, eventually they will become the thing you feared. Thomasin was faithful. She didn't want to turn to the devil or become a witch but she was judged and punished by her family, often for other issues they couldn't be honest about like her mother's obvious resentment of her, and eventually when left with nothing, she had only one real option - to become the thing they chose to believe she was. Her family drove her to Black Philip because of their fear and judgement and in so doing, they have ensured the perpetuation of the type of scenario they found themselves in. So you can look at it as her finding a sense of freedom and in a sense she does because she's no longer beholden to society's norms but it's also a tragedy because if her family had just listened and believed her, they might not have ended up the way they did. Although they had faith, their beliefs were so rigid that they couldn't see the truth when it was right in front of their faces, namely that the twins were the ones they should have been worried about."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "They blame the family for their own downfall because of their strict religious beliefs and superstitions. But they where right all along… The devil and other evil beings clearly exist in their universe, which the movie establishes in the first ten minutes. By extension that means that the families fear of the devil and strict devotion was completely reasonable. Also most of the bad things that happened were out of their control. Their family goat was literally satan incarcerated, so they we’re probably screwed from the start anyways."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"I think Thomasin’s innocence is the main point of the film, she is condemned by her family out of their stubbornness and pride (a sin) rather than by any actual evidence.

And I disagree with your Jaws analogy if you were to use Jaws then it’s more like Bruce never actually kills anyone throughout the entire film but is nevertheless eventually hunted down by the boat and so is forced to kill them in self-defence. Then Bruce, being treated like a killer, swims off to join the pack of sharks.

The thing you misunderstand about the coven’s appearance is that it is not a part of the main story, it is a part of the denouement (to translate, it’s Thomasin’s new normal.)

It’s almost as if the theme of the movie is “if you’ve done the time might as well do the crime.”"
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The film is about the danger of obsessive piety and how slavish devotion to a doctrine is not only stifling, but self-destructive.

The family is exiled from the community because of their hardline attitudes on religion. Then the family slowly disintegrates from within as their obsession with avoiding evil and sin transforms every misfortune into a willful attack by the “forces of evil”.

While Thomasin seems to be cognizant of the fanaticism of her family she doesn’t really participate in it. She serves as the audience watching the events unfold, unsure if they are just a string of bad luck or something more intentionally malicious.

Each other family member displays some facet of the seven deadly sins:

Father : pride Mother : wrath & greed Brother : lust Twins : Sloth & Envy

As result they fall from grace and are dispatched, in part, as a result of their hysteria.

At the end only Thomasin remains and we are shown that, indeed, The Devil has been around the whole time stoking and poking the fire.

With nothing left she gives in to temptation and sheds her former self, but is ultimately damned as well."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "how do you have twins, get divorced, and then decide that you each get sole custody of a daughter and never mention to them that they have a twin? and the parents act like the other twin doesn’t exist. how do you voluntarily become a deadbeat parent? what kind of parents are they? they lied to their kids their whole entire life. and how did the girls not know from jump when they met each other that they are twins? they literally have the same face loll. these parents were willing to not see their other child anymore cause they simply don’t like each other. "
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Director Liza Johnson needs to be given a lot of credit, not only for her obvious rapport with actors (and skill in bringing the best out of them on camera - the whole cast is superb), but the balance and perspective she brings to the material. This film is a comedy (and an effective one) about what is, on its face, a truly absurd moment in history - yet she always maintains an essential affection for her characters and respect for their dignity, never stooping for cheap laughs or settling for oversimplification. Her timing is sharp, her staging direct and effective. In short, she does a damn good job and I'm interested in seeing more of her work."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The Nancy Meyers effect at it's best. She took a cute, campy preteen girl movie from the 60s and turned it into a cinematic, larger than life romantic comedy. All her movies bring a certain ostensible sense of action and movement. I don't know if it's her tendency to physically move her characters around to different settings, setup big sequences like the fencing, the hotel rendezvous, or even thinking of Somethings Gotta Give and the sequence of the dinner in Paris. Maybe it's the tension she puts into the stories and constant build up to a reveal or climax. Either way, she's a master in driving plot and using beautiful imagery, settings, and acting to elevate every scene."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Yes yes. I have a whole rant I've given on this. Freaky Friday is a silly premise and script but the acting is a masterclass. The way Lindsey and Jamie are able to do each others' mannerisms once they've switched. While making the whole "middle aged woman in a teen body" and vice versa just also fucking hysterical and believable. Two amazing talents in top form, playing on each other. Fantastic."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Alfonso Cuaron was the only director who I felt really added anything interesting to the franchise - he didn't just blandly translate the book to film exactly how you would expect. He made it into something unique and interesting, something with an actual style and a feeling and emotion to the world."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "What surprised me most was how excellent the script was. It drew extremely intelligent characters with clear motivations and characterizations. Perhaps most impressively was how it seemed every detail introduced in the script had a pay-off. Powell's story about shooting the kid? Paid off by him saving John at the end. Take your shoes off and make fists with your toes? Oops, now you have glass in your feet because you don't have shoes. Marriage problems causing your wife to go by her maiden name? Conceals her identity from an excellent thief, buying John more time. Every single detail was important and had a purpose."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The second film had the unenviable task of following up its masterpiece predecessor. The film is often accused of being too similar to the original film with McClane once again finding himself in the wrong place at the wrong time, a point that is even humorously pointed out by McClane in the film. Despite the criticism the film does actually have its own identity and look, with Renny Harlin directing instead of John McTiernan. The villain's plan is quite different and the twist with Major Grant is particularly effective. It's an above average action movie, that doesn't live up to the standard set by the first film."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"John McTiernan returned for the 3rd film which feels like a direct sequel to the original film. The burnt out McClane is one of Willis greatest performances and his chemistry with Samuel L Jackson is incredible. Jeremy Irons is a fantastic villain and an example of some of the greatest casting of all time. I can't think of anyone better to follow up Alan Rickman's Hans Gruber than Jeremy Irons with his equally unique voice and presence.

The fourth film was poor and disappointing while the 5th film was an unwatchable abomination. Willis portrayal of McClane in both made him feel like a different character, while the vulnerability seen in the original trilogy is almost completely absent."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "John McTeirnan did a great job at directing this. Most of the action is in your face and very well filmed you don’t get shaky cam at all and he did very well at getting a good fun movie."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Plus Bruce Willis was perfect for this role. He had the badass look but he is completely human. Bruce Willis does very well for playing this character. And as well as Bruce Willis Alan Rickman was great too. He did really great as Hans and he had that weird vibe of like “alright, this guys a creep”"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Alan Rickman (RIP) should always get his flowers for his contributions to Die Hard, and the screenplay itself has been copied many times over. It was rich with characters from the limo driver to Al Powell, "dwayne" and agent Johnson and Johnson (no relation)... little moments of the bad ass swat team guy getting hit by a thorn lol....its a very rich screenplay that's one of a kind"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "“Disclosure Day” is an old man’s movie. I don’t mean that in a derogatory or ageist way; I mean that this is a film the director could not have made in his younger days. Because every skill he acquired back then is now being used to shape and inspire his most recent work. There’s the perspective of a wiser"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Dwayne Johnson delivers the best performance of his career as the amiable but troubled UFC champion Mark Kerr. Emily Blunt and Ryan Bader are also excellent in their roles. The screenplay is repetitive and frustrating. Blunt's character is so unlikeable and written with such vitriol that it becomes exhausting to watch her, although Blunt's performance is as good as it could possibly be."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Johnson has rarely played a loser, but he’s always been likable, displaying a massive grin to match his massive pecs in action vehicles that never allowed him to showcase much range. He manages to go deep here without overdoing it, killing the audience with kindness as a benign warrior who suffers from one scene to the next, triumphing briefly in the ring before succumbing to addiction and/or romantic grief. Like Mickey Rourke in The Wrestler — a film from which Safdie seems to take a few cues — the actor delivers an intoxicating mix of blood, sweat, tears, protein and total helplessness."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Dwayne Johnson owns the whole thing with his truly remarkable work as fighter Mark Kerr, disappearing so fully underneath Kazu Hiru’s astonishing prosthetics that the opening of the film, presented as contemporary footage from an event in Sao Paulo 1997, looks genuinely like the real thing. It’s that rare beast, a biopic that’s light on the bio and resistant to being a pic. It’s a film about a human being, and its effect is strangely haunting, since Dwayne Johnson seems to do everything while doing nothing."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "“Oppenheimer” tacks on a trendy doomsday message about how the world was destroyed by nuclear weapons. But if Oppenheimer, in his way, made the bomb all about him, by that point it’s Nolan and his movie who are doing the same thing."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "A biopic in constant free fall, Oppenheimer is Christopher Nolan’s most abstract yet most exacting work, with themes of guilt writ-large through apocalyptic IMAX nightmares that grow both more enormous and more intimate as time ticks on. A disturbing, mesmerizing vision of what humanity is capable of bringing upon itself, both through its innovation, and through its capacity to justify any atrocity."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Stylistically, “Oppenheimer” recalls Oliver Stone's "JFK" in the way it weaves together important history and significant side players, and while it doesn't hit the same emotional notes as Nolan's inspired "Interstellar," the film succeeds as both character study and searing cautionary tale about taking science too far. Characters from yesteryear worry about nervously pushing a fateful button and setting the world on fire, although Nolan drives home the point that fiery existential threat could reignite any time now."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "For me, Dunkirk is supposed to put you in the shoes of a soldier on that beach, unaware of who the men around you are, unaware of what's going on, but just ensued in chaos. It's experiential, and more kind of an exploration of the frantic nature of what happened at Dunkirk than it is about individual characters or arcs."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Honestly Depp’s portrayal of Wonka was the most off-putting part of the Burton movie. The pristine bob haircut, the clean shaven face; his pallid, slightly waxy complexion, topped with obvious contempt for the children, daddy issues and unnerving mannerisms- all of this just creeped people out. Depp explained he wanted to act like a cynical game show host, but I would say he drew far more comparisons to Michael Jackson (though he denies any connection). Depp’s Wonka doesn’t really seem sincere at all and even though Charlie wins the prize, they added in a whole bunch of bullshit about Wonka reconnecting with his dad before the movie can be drawn to a close. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Wilder’s Wonka is largely why the ‘71 movie works- he is definitely bizarre, cynical and also shows contempt for the horrid kids and their parents like Depp, but he seems like more of a genuinely gentle and warm person deep down. The look in his eyes is a man who is world-weary but passionate about his craft and what it means. The final scene where he tells Charlie that he won and embraces him feels like a man who has rekindled his hope in humanity by finding a genuinely good child to continue his legacy. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"First of all, the kids are all better developed in Charlie. In the original they are pretty interchangeable in terms of personality. One is a annoying and chews gum, one is annoying and watches tv, and one is annoying and fat. Veruca stands out a bit because she gets a song, but is she also mostly just annoying and obnoxious. In the newer version they show Violet as an overly competitive sore winner/loser and Mike as a know-it-all who's constantly questioning the point of anything Wonka is doing.

I really like the scene near the end in the remake where you see everyone else leaving the factory. Each child and parent pair has a quick conversation that I think sums up the idea that the reason the kids all suck is their parents enabling and encouraging them to be assholes. And it's the parents that walk away from the factory having learned their lesson, not the kids. To me that ties in really well with the whole concept of Wonka not wanting an adult to run his factory, because they suck."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"Much like Sigourney Waver rose to fame in the late 70's, 80's and into the 90's doing a mix of sci-fi, action, and drama roles and excelling at all of them, Emily Blunt seems to be the modern version of that from the late 2000's and especially the 2010's onward.

Emily Blunt in sci-fi films like Looper, Edge of Tomorrow, A Quiet Place, action films like Fall Guys and Sicario, fun movies like Disney's Mary Poppin's reboot and The Jungle Cruise, drama roles like Oppenheimer and Devil Wears Prada 2.

Like I can't help but feel she carved out the same niche for herself that Sigourney Weaver had in the 70's, 80's and 90's. And Emily did this WITHOUT being cast in superhero films like Marvel or DC which would feel like an easy way in for her, she did it all with original roles"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Emily Blunt definitely has range but Sigourney was doing something completely different in her era. Like Ripley was this massive cultural shift - before Alien you didnt really see women carrying action films like that. Emily is great but shes working in landscape where strong female leads are already established you know"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Blunt’s filmography is impressive, and almost inconceivable it took so long to be honored with an Academy Award nomination. Her remarkable body of work has earned her six Golden Globe noms, two BAFTA nods, and four Screen Actors Guild nominations. She notably clinched a SAG Award for her compelling portrayal of a pregnant mother navigating a post-apocalyptic world in “A Quiet Place,” directed by her husband, John Krasinski. This victory made her as only the second actor to win a SAG Award without receiving an Oscar nomination, following Idris Elba for “Beasts of No Nation” in 2015."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The indie dramedy from the late Lynn Shelton gives Blunt a moment to not just show her superb acting skills and range, but how beautifully she works with those around her, in this case Rosemarie DeWitt and Mark Duplass, who also shine. Unconventional in its narrative structure, the film’s purity is felt through the passion of the cast."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Prior to Christopher Nolan’s Dark Knight trilogy, films adapted from comics were a largely uplifting affair full of primary colors and bright locations. It’s easy to forget how much had to go right for “Batman Begins” to succeed, but it started with finding a villain who was as compelling as his nemesis. Though Murphy originally tested for Bruce Wayne/Batman, it was a stroke of genius that Nolan would peg him as Dr. Jonathan Crane, aka Scarecrow. With a charisma that can be both terrifying and seductive (which would become a Murphy speciality in years to come) Crane doesn’t need to showboat he’s the bad guy. Rather, he exudes a calm confidence, taking his time with methodical precision. Even the way he says the word “Batman,” drawing it out into two separate words, is chilling. It was wise of Nolan to include Scarecrow in the film’s two sequels — particularly in “The Dark Knight Rises,” where he pops up as a judge in a kangaroo courtroom with a smirk (“Exile or death?”) that shows he’s enjoying this almost as much as the audience is."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Murphy has always been drawn to the written word, no matter the medium. So after headlining films from the likes of Danny Boyle and Neil Jordan, he made a point to return to the stage and had no qualms about signing up for a television series penned by acclaimed writer Steven Knight (“Dirty Pretty Things,” “Eastern Promises”). The actor is a perfect fit in Knight’s universe of morally compromised men you can’t take your eyes off of. His Tommy Shelby is a war veteran turned leader of the Peaky Blinders criminal organization. His work is all pragmatism: He’s often stoic as he manipulates and calculates. But he’s also haunted — both by what he’s seen in the war and by his deep and abiding love for his late wife Grace. Of course, Murphy is so magnetic, fans of the show often have to be reminded that he is actually a villain. Put aside the mass murder and corruption — I’m still not over him shooting that horse."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "When citing Murphy’s collaborations with Nolan, it would make sense to favor “Dunkirk,” in which he offers a harrowing depiction of PTSD as a traumatized solider; or “Oppenheimer,” in which he occupies nearly every frame as the star of the film. But for me, Murphy is the lynchpin in one of Nolan’s best, most ambitious and most emotional masterpieces, the byzantine thriller “Inception.” He plays Robert Michael Fischer, the heir to a business empire whose unresolved daddy issues make him the target of a team of “extractors.” Led by Leonardo DiCaprio’s Dom Cobb, the thieves use dream technology to infiltrate the subconscious of a target to access information. Fischer is a man of few words and Murphy is fantastic at quietly communicating his pain and making you care for a character that is, in many ways, intended to be a cipher. Murphy shows that “best” doesn’t need to mean “most” — both in terms of the size of the role and the acting, delivering a sublime supporting performance that the entire movie rests on. Nolan is often accused (wrongly) of being a cold filmmaker. But the moment where Fischer finds closure with his late father is perhaps the most affecting gut-punch he’s ever delivered, thanks largely to Murphy’s beautiful performance."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "However, the true brilliance of Witherspoon’s performance is how she digs to find the textured depth in Woods. She has pressing vulnerabilities, endearing charm, and a steadfast application, and her drive to excel to prove herself worthy of her ex’s affection sees her achieve far more than her self-doubt ever let her believe she could. With Legally Blonde concluding as a feel-good story of self-discovery, Witherspoon’s performance is a masterclass in tonal balance, a delicate yet impressionable juggling of hilarity and heartfelt femininity. Elle Woods is one of the most iconic and inspiring characters we have seen this century, one that continues to be of relevance 25 years later."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Masterfully blurring the lines between mentor and abuser with an unforgettable intensity and might, J. K. Simmons’ imposing performance as jazz instructor Terrence Fletcher imbues Whiplash with much of its assertive brilliance. The film follows Andrew’s (Miles Teller) tumultuous time at a prestigious conservatory where he pursues his dream of becoming an acclaimed drummer under the tutelage of Fletcher. Whiplash explores destructive obsession with fascinating detail while emphasizing the complexity of the central relationship in which Andrew resents Fletcher yet yearns for his approval."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Within this framework, Simmons puts in a truly perfect performance, realizing Fletcher’s somewhat contradictory nature and unpredictable volatility with enormous impact. While the character’s methods are questionable, there is a distinct idea behind his approach that insists on pushing his students to achieve greatness by any means necessary, even if that means inflicting psychological and physical torment upon them. The nuance and passion that Simmons brings to the character make Fletcher one of the most compelling antagonists cinema has seen this century."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"While it isn’t an archetype unique to 21st-century cinema, thriller films of the past 25 years have had a particular penchant for psychotic antagonists, with one of the best being Gone Girl’s Amy Dunne. Played to perfection in a career-defining performance from Rosamund Pike, Dunne is the chilling embodiment of calculated evil. This manipulative and fiercely intelligent psychopath's efforts to make her distant husband pay for his adulterous indifference lead to an elaborate case of missing persons misdirection that nearly sees Nick Dunne (Ben Affleck) accused of her murder.

While Amy is defined by her relentless drive and her vitriolic sense of indignation, Pike still imbues her with profound depth that prevails in her wry sense of dark humor and almost begrudging sense of obligation to Nick when he practically begs for her help. Seeing her transition from her easy-going “cool girl” façade to her ruthless and vindictive outrage is a wicked joy to watch. While Gone Girl features Gillian Flynn’s brilliant screenplay (based on her novel) and David Fincher’s masterful direction, it is truly Pike’s savage and sinister portrayal of Amy that makes the film the instant classic of thriller cinema that it is."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "One of the brightest stars in cinematic history, Meryl Streep has long stood among Hollywood’s most decorated and acclaimed actresses, with an astonishing 21 Academy Award nominations and three wins. While it isn’t among her Oscar-winning roles, her performance as Miranda Priestly in the fashion industry comedy-drama The Devil Wears Prada is a masterpiece of real-world villainy. She revels in the decadent, devilish divinity of playing so callous and career-minded a character but never fails to underline Miranda’s icy nature with an alluring complexity that illustrates the pressures of the industry and hints at a tumultuous personal past that has made her the way she is."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Able to convey enormous emotions with the slightest facial twitches, Streep’s performance is an astounding feat of acting as an emotionally reserved character, while her delivery of lines that do directly illustrate her mood is often striking and quite funny. The embodiment of high standards and complete authority, Streep dominates the film with graceful ease, using mere inflections of her voice to illustrate Miranda’s commanding influence on her work and employees. She is undeniably intimidating, even when delivering her funniest lines, but she brings so much more to the role of Miranda Priestly than just a villainous tyrant, making for one of the great performances of the century so far."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Bridesmaids made Kristen Wiig an Oscar-nominated talent, but, interestingly, it was for her screenwriting—alongside Annie Mumolo—rather than her lead performance. While the weighty and contemplative comedy is beautifully written, Wiig’s resonant, richly layered, and wonderfully complicated portrayal of Annie Walker is arguably the film’s shimmering highlight. With the delightfully debauchery-laden comedy following Annie’s efforts to make her long-time best friend’s wedding day everything she dreamed of, Wiig is able to rely on her charismatic charm and grounded appeal, especially through the first half, which functions as a stream of vivaciously vulgar scenarios."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "However, with Annie also harboring angst over her failed business and unfulfilling romantic life, Wiig must also imbue the character with a wave of depressive waywardness that only bubbles to the surface as her grasp on wedding planning is gradually ripped away from her. Within this juxtaposition of raucous and raunchy comedy and heartachingly raw drama, Wiig’s performance strikes a flawless balance that is rich with pathos and pain. It sees Bridesmaids achieve far more than the average movie, and is a significant reason it has endured as one of the best and funniest comedies of the decade."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Baz Luhrmann’s take on the F. Scott Fitzgerald classic is a mixed bag for some. However, DiCaprio’s portrayal of the enigmatic and charismatic Jay Gatsby, a mysterious millionaire known for his extravagant parties and undying love for Daisy Buchanan (played by Carey Mulligan), is a common denominator of praise. He effectively conveyed Gatsby’s longing for lost love and his relentless pursuit of the American Dream, making the relatable and tragic story feel modern."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Leonardo DiCaprio takes on a subject dear to his heart (climate crisis) but executes it in a way we haven’t seen in any other roles. As Dr. Randall Mindy, an astronomer who, along with his colleague Kate Dibiasky (played courageously by Jennifer Lawrence), tries desperately to inform the world of its impending doom, he stretches out in both his dramatic and comedic moments. His chemistry with Brie Evantee (played expertly by Cate Blanchett) is one of the film’s brighter spots in an otherwise very bleak look at where our species will inevitably end up (unless things begin to change)."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "So much of this movie is understood by looking at Greta Lee’s face. Her character’s feelings, thoughts, memories, overt and covert emotions. She conveys all of this and more in a magnetic performance. It’s a love story between her and these two men, but’s also a love story to Korea, to Canada, to New York, and to the story of an immigrant."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The first of three Tarantino movies on the list and by far the least appreciated one. You spend nearly every scene with Jackson and it’s hard to look away. I always think about Hateful Eight as a play you’d see in a theatre because 90% of the movie is set in one room. The bit about the Lincoln Letter is fantastic and shows you how good he is at playing others, which he does consistently throughout the movie. There’s something Knives Out-y about the movie and his performance, which I mean as a high compliment."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Sean Baker’s movie builds an American story of poverty, childhood, adulthood that at times makes you feel cynical. Dafoe is a ray of light within this dark arena, reminding viewers of the age old adage “it takes a village.” His performance makes me emotional because it feels like there’s so much buried inside his character that we don’t know about. But what we see, we love."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "This movie answers the question “What if we just really pushed it to the limit drinking wise?” The answer is the one you know was always coming. Mads portrays drinking and alcohol as it is — highs of highs, lows of lows. Speaking of lows, there is something tragic about Mikkelsen’s characters (not just this one) that is challenging to describe but feels deeply connected to his eyes."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Before watching Tár, I didn’t feel confident that the movie would hook me. Classical music, drama with the orchestra, Germany — just not my main interests. Once you start, though, you can’t take your eyes of it and that’s mostly because you spend every scene with Cate Blanchett. She slowly starts unraveling. It’s a movie about power and the audience slowly sees her power fade away as the character dissolves. Her performance feels so measured and true. More so than most others on this list, you feel like she is the character."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Another movie with multiple great main characters, but, to me, Brolin’s character is the heart of the movie. He’s the one experiencing real change, real fear, real regret. The other two stay somewhat flat despite mesmerizing performances. A few favorite moments of his performance — laying in bed thinking about whether to bring water back to the guy, smashing the phone after talking to Chigur, laying in the hospital talking to Woody Harrelson’s character."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The Martian is a movie about — one — the enduring nature of the human spirit and — two — how charismatic Matt Damon is. He’s stranded, not playing off of anyone but himself, essentially filming a vlog for the majority of the film. To be honest, the parts without him (like back on earth with Jeff Daniels, sorry Jeff) are significantly less enjoyable than when you’re hanging out with Botanist Mark Watney and he’s telling you about irrigation. There were many space-centric movies in the first 25 years of this millennium, but Damon’s performance in The Martian is my favorite of them all (yes, even that one)."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Does it matter that Jack Black is essentially playing himself? I don’t think so. There are a other movies on this list that involve performing with child actors, but Black’s is the most energetic (though still grounded in honesty and compassion). He plays off of them so well like a good teacher, tapping into different emotions for different students."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "When I think about this movie, all I think about is Sandra Hüller. I can hear her voice, pitched high and shaky. I spent the whole time trying to look behind her large blue eyes thinking “Did she do it?” and never arriving at an answer. At times, it feels like you’re watching a true crime documentary and Hüller is the real life suspect. Because of her performance, there’s something so unsettling about this movie that I don’t even want to revisit scenes."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "In my humble opinion, Lady Bird is one of the most important movies of the 2010s. Greta Gerwig wrote a masterpiece and Saoirse Ronan brought it to life with her humor, wit, and awkwardness. Ronan gets my award, but we have to give an honorable mention to Laurie Metcalf (Lady Bird’s mom) because she’s the other half of this powerful equation. The way this movie depicts the mother-daughter relationship is fantastic, and I say that as neither a mother nor a daughter!"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The first time you watch Fiennes play M. Gustave, you are are left remembering how silly of a character he is. His quick quips, his obsession with older women , the bit about how much cologne he wears. Fiennes delivery is perfect as is the way he puts emphasis on saying each syllable. But if you watch GBP again and again like I have, you start to see the sadness in M. Gustave. He takes meals alone in his room (a servant’s quarters with a tiny bed) and we have no idea of his backstory. There’s insecurity there, masked by a lot of vanity and grooming."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "For a movie where the two main characters are suffering from mental illness, Silver Linings Playbook is oddly hopeful and that’s in large part due to Jennifer Lawrence. She appears as this dark and twisted side character but transforms into the real engine of the plot. She’s consistently the only honest one and you grow to feel so attached to her through the watch."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "McDormand plays a grieving mom in this Martin McDonough-directed movie. Based on that description, you might think her character easy to root for. She is not. You understand her motivations, but some of her actions are hard to defend. She’s complicated, but redeemable. What sticks in my mind is McDormand’s perfect line reading is perfect like when she’s talking to the priest who visits her in the kitchen."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"One way you can analyze Heath Ledger’s Joker character is by comparing him to other star villains. Unlike Anton Chigur, the Joker follows no code or principal. Unlike Voldemort, he has no history or weakness we know of. Unlike Alonzo in Training Day, his back isn’t against the wall. Unlike Amy Dunne, there seem to be no long term strategy. Joker tells us this in the movie (“Do I really look like a guy with a plan?”) and Alfred reinforces it to Bruce (“Some men just want to watch the world burn”).

Ledger takes a cartoon character part from a super hero movie and turns it into one of the most frightening and captivating performances I’ve ever seen."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Christoph Waltz, an actor largely unkown to American audiences until this moment, steps out of a Volkswagen in Nazi-occupied France and extends his hand. The rest is history. Waltz plays Colonel Hans Landa, an SS operative nicknamed the “Jew Hunter.” If you haven’t seen the movie, that might sound pretty dark. Landa is, of course, but that doesn’t mean it isn’t a delight to watch Waltz portray him. In that opening scene, there’s a five-second moment of stillness where his face changes as Hans Landa goes from friend to foe. No point in me writing about it, see for yourself."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"It’s the little things like this that make it memorable — the way he lights his pipe at LaPadite’s table, the way he overprounces “Au Revoir, Sho-sha-na!” and the way he says “Bravo!” to the Basterd introducing himself as Dominic Decoco at the premier. In the strudel scene, he is superb. Every time I watch, I ask myself the same question: “Does he know that’s Shoshana sitting across from him?” The answer is likely no, but his energy is so intimidating that you just aren’t sure.

In the end, not only is Landa evil and irredeemable, but he contains no true allegiance. He sells out the soon-to-be-doomed Nazis to save himself and earn a house on Nantucket. His ending, the end of the movie, is the best possible punishment for a man such as himself. Not death, something better. A masterpiece.

Press enter or click to view image in full size"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Segel’s physical comedy alone could get him on this list. The way he carries himself in this movie, so often slumped over or looking gangly and awkward. That should take nothing away from his hilarious dialogue with the hotel staff, his whining, his crying, his Rock Opera singing."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I realized this is Mike’s story and the movie started to focus on him, it all just started to click for me. Even going back to the very first sequence in the film, when Mike said “that’s my family, it’s not me”, everything just came full circle in such an incredible way. Amazing performances across the board, great cinematography, soundtrack, editing, etc… and I honestly think Al Pacino gave one of the single greatest performances in history. The final scene was just perfect from an acting perspective. He plays the young college guy just as good as he plays the godfather mob boss. You can literally see it in his eyes, the change that he goes through in the film. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I realized this is Mike’s story and the movie started to focus on him, it all just started to click for me. Even going back to the very first sequence in the film, when Mike said “that’s my family, it’s not me”, everything just came full circle in such an incredible way. Amazing performances across the board, great cinematography, soundtrack, editing, etc… and I honestly think Al Pacino gave one of the single greatest performances in history. The final scene was just perfect from an acting perspective. He plays the young college guy just as good as he plays the godfather mob boss. You can literally see it in his eyes, the change that he goes through in the film. "
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"The Godfather and The Godfather II are the best movies ever made. I view both of them as one of the same films.

When you check out Part II and look to watch Part III, you will see a lot of discourse about the drop in quality. My advice is to go in with as open a mind as possible, it is a muuuchhh better film than it is made out to be. It just has the greatest films ever created to compete with."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "The reason I am saying is that The Godfather certainly is not an easy movie. There are many layers one should understand both for the story and cinematography. Thus, this shocked me since average audience usually do not want to watch 3 hours movie with somehow more art house than the rest of the American cinema. Yet, I will admit that I am a bit lost about the term “art house”. So my first question is how would you define art house and what makes a movie fall under that category? Because I have been thinking about this question for a while and my mind associates art house movies mostly with non-English movies if not mostly with European movies. I know this is not the only criteria that makes movies art house. I’m sure we can all list art house American movies. Yet, wouldn’t you say that majority of art house is outside of US? And secondly, would you consider The Godfather an art house movie? Because I would certainly do. (Again I do not know the actual definition so I might be completely wrong.)"
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"They are surprisingly great!

I just watched the whole series back-to-back for the first time a year or so ago. I'm not interested in boxing, but I'd always heard they were good and I was really impressed and now I'd count several of them among my favorite films. They're not about boxing really, but rather a character study of an interesting guy through practically his whole life.

Here's my quick non-spoilery breakdown on overall quality. Rocky is a classic and a great film. Rocky II is a bit of a rehash but good nonetheless. Rocky III has probably the best villain. Rocky IV is pure 80s Cold War cheese but a lot of fun. Rocky V is the weakest of the series, but easy enough to get through. Rocky Balboa is my personal favorite. I wasn't expecting it to be any good but it was fantastic, and Creed was very impressive. Can't wait for the sequel."
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"This is one of my oldest and most-cherished comfort movies. I don’t even know how old I was when I first saw it, but very very young.

I loved the music, the goofy premise, the whole summer camp, the international settings: this movie just captivated young me and will never fail to make me smile. I don’t remember ever seriously dreaming of moving abroad before seeing it, it may have had a real influence on the path I chose.

It‘s"
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"Home Alone 3 is, no joke, the one of the three that I watch the most. It's not the best, but it's the most ridiculous and cartoon-like and somehow still works. Plus, I love the fact that the main character's family actually likes him. Like, yeah, his siblings bully him (because they are siblings), but they show up for him at the end.

Also trips me out that Scarlett Johansson plays the sister. This was the first movie I saw her in and probably the reason why I had a crush on her through the 2000s."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Monty Python and the Holy Grail. The absurdist humour never fails to make me laugh. It is all just ridiculous and that is why it works, right from the opening credits and the moose jokes until the very end."
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"Miss March - that movie is considered as bad movie, but I don’t care because it’s funny to me and that’s enough.
