Say it isn’t so - That is a really underrated comedy movie and one of the funniest movies I have ever seen."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Ricky Stanicky was the funniest movie I’ve seen in a long time, directed by one of the Farrelly brothers so made sense once the credits rolled. Fits right in with their movies"
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I've watched Into the Wild and Captain fantastic and they're both awesome movies. I spent the week after watching them rethinking all of my life's choices"
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I started my film binge with the right step forward. The newest adaptation of Frankenstein was modernly original yet an accurate representation of Mary Shelley’s Frankenstein.I’ve actually read Mary Shelley’s novel, which was beneficial, and I better understood the value in smaller things. The best way to describe this was “pretty.” Every aspect had the visual appeal of a gothic aesthetic. If I knew film terminology, I would have a much better word than “pretty,” but I assume my direction is understood. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Even if I wasn’t on a film binge, I still would have gone out of my way to watch Wicked: For Good. Yes, I am biased because of my love for musical theatre, but the Wicked films will go down as some of the most famous films from the 21st century. The talent is unreal. It’s hard to fathom that it’s humanly possible to sing like that. I fail to figure out people who are suspiciously good at something. I’m sure everyone is done with hearing about Wicked. In short, if you haven’t seen it, please do. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "A film I had such high hopes for was just disappointing. It missed my expectations. It seemed like the film was portrayed more as a drama than a comedy (it should have been a comedy). The cast of Dakota Johnson, Chris Evans and Pedro Pascal had a great opportunity to recreate the 2000s rom-com craze, but they were nowhere close. I wouldn’t advise watching this movie — I would like to save fellow viewers from feelings of disappointment."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I’ve learned that Wes Anderson is my favorite director. His approach to film is so original. I can always tell when I’m watching a Wes Anderson movie and I like that. The animation and visual aspects of the film were so intriguing, it made me feel safe. It’s whimsy and fun. I related to it in more ways than I would have expected. I understood it as a movie for the people who don’t always fit in. Through the world of Fantastic Mr. Fox, all the characters feel like misfits and are as confused about life as you are. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This may be an unpopular opinion, but I usually can’t stand heartwarming high school movies. Maybe it’s because I hated high school, but this film was so charming to watch. It gave me the same feelings as Fantastic Mr. Fox. The tone of the movie felt comforting, and it was refreshing to see the main character, “Juno,” being able to handle a teen pregnancy with such grace. A plot that could easily be dramatic was so lighthearted and lovely. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "The Goonies is a classic movie everyone is bound to watch throughout their lives. Honestly, I can’t find myself watching this again, but if it ever comes on with a group I wouldn’t complain. It’s relieving that I’ve checked this one off my list — I do understand the appeal as it’s charming and heart warming, a movie to watch with family."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I love ‘90s and 2000s rom-coms because they’re cheesy and predictable but in the best way possible. After watching, I wouldn’t say it’s my favorite rom-com but it follows the classic cliche of the popular guy asking out the nerdy art girl and then she becomes popular. I hate the glamorization of popularity in high school, especially in these types of movies, but I still love watching them because the ridiculousness of it is comical."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This is the type of movie you leave feeling speechless, not in the shocked way but in the sad way. I wish the movie ended on a happier note but I do understand why the ending was the way it was. That being said, I wouldn’t want the ending to be changed. It’s a better representation of the purpose of the story. I have an appreciation for movies like Dead Poets Society — it tells a sad story with grace."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This may be cheating because I’ve already watched this movie but my excuse was it was Christmas time. There’s not much I have to say about this film besides the good memories associated with it. Growing up, this was always my favorite version of The Grinch, some may disagree but I find the animation to be sentimental."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I didn’t love Baby Driver, it’s not for me. I’m not sure what I was expecting but I did think I would like it more. I do have to say, the music aspect of the film was really interesting. From the little I know about movies, it appeared to be done very well, but something felt missing to me. Maybe it was because the lead’s name was “Baby” and that somehow made me resentful."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This was a movie I shouldn’t have watched before going to bed. For those who are unaware, the film is about a romance surrounding two cannibals. Before watching, I knew Bones and All was about cannibalism, I just didn’t expect to see everything I saw. If blood freaks you out, I beg of you not to watch this. I consider myself to be fine around graphic images but there were a few instances of getting up and taking a lap around my living room to collect myself. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "The Truman Show was a movie I knew the ending of prior to watching. For that reason, I never took the time to watch it. I think if the ending wasn’t spoiled for me I would have enjoyed it a lot more or have been more engaged. If you have zero clue what this movie is about I suggest watching it now. For that reason, I don’t want to spoil anything. The Truman Show does mess with your head and arguably your perspective on life."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This movie checked off the majority of my main interests: music and journalism. Evidently I adored this movie, I was in awe the whole time. This is the type of movie you leave wanting to start a whole new persona about. Anyone who is into music should watch this movie, no matter the genres you’re into. It’s such an ode to the lifestyle and rush of being a musician. I will be watching this many more times throughout my lifetime. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I watched Step Brothers with some of my closest childhood friends, and I think all of us would say we found it very funny. I’ve noticed I struggle to have a laugh more than a giggle while watching movies but I do feel like I confidently laughed at a couple scenes. Will Ferrell is a big factor to why I found Step Brothers so funny. There’s no better way to describe Will Ferrell as just a universally funny person."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Shutter Island is a classic Leonardo DiCaprio film. It was dramatic and exciting while defining himself as a respectable actor. Whoever came up with the plot of this film is a genius. It’s shocking and messes with your head in a very real way. This genre of film isn’t something I find myself always going out of my way to watch. If you can’t tell, I love lighthearted films but considering this, I’m happy I took the time to watch it. I doubt I’ll watch it again, but I’m happy to know the storyline."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "My love for Wes Anderson will continue to grow. This movie emphasizes the unspoken impact of mental health. It represents a flawed family in such a unique way but yet still feels relatable. I usually don’t notice the casting details in movies, but this film was cast perfectly in my opinion. I will be rewatching this film in the near future. It’s a somewhat dark story, yet so comforting. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I watched this movie with my grandma. We both found it funny, but I wouldn’t suggest watching it with any older relative or relative, to say the least. This would be a great movie for a night in with friends, it is funny with crude humor. The film is a mockumentary on the philosophy and science of life with “Philomena Cunk” as the narrator. She explains human life in such a uniquely odd and humorous way. Throughout the film she interviews highly respected professors and scientists asking them the most stupid questions. It’s hard to tell if they understand she’s joking and they give the best responses. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "It was cute, nothing special but as a rom-com lover I can find a way to enjoy it. It’s based on the book Regretting You by Colleen Hoover. She also had another movie adaptation for her book It Ends With Us. If I had to pick between the two movies, Regretting You is by far better. This is a movie I would have loved to watch at a girls’ night sleepover in middle school, which is the simplest way I can describe it. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Before this, I’ve never seen a film in the Japanese animation genre. Ponyo has been on my watch list for the longest time. I’ve never gotten around to it, but the opportunity perfectly presents itself as of now. I adored this film, the animation was what did it for me. It felt very personal and an ode to childhood imaginations. I can see this becoming a comfort movie for me and soon getting into a hyperfixation of other Japanese animated films, Spirited Away is on the list now. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Watching Interstellar on a flight made sense to me and added to my viewing experience with some occasional turbulence. I didn’t know Timotheé Chalamet was in the film, which took me off guard. Throughout the film, I kept thinking, “How didn’t I know he was in this?” I hate that this thought completely distracted me from several parts of the movie, but when I was paying full attention I loved watching it. I don’t mean to disappoint those who were expecting more from my thoughts on Interstellar, but I’m sending the reminder that I know little to nothing about film. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "The last episode of Stranger Things played in theaters, so I figured to add it to my reviews. Even though it’s technically not a “film,” Stranger Things was very present throughout my break. I’ve spent my teenage years obsessing over Stranger Things, so the dramatic span of releasing episodes felt warranted. I’ve heard a lot of conversation surrounding the ending, mainly about people not liking it. At first I liked the ending, but after sitting on it for a while I’ve grown more to dislike it. I think everyone was expecting a sadder ending. Collectively we prepared to be upset but then things worked out, which was oddly disappointing. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I assume by the films on this list, you can understand I’m not the biggest horror fan. I knew I needed to include at least one and after scrolling through some streaming platforms, I landed on Bodies Bodies Bodies. I wouldn’t consider this as an actual scary movie, I found myself laughing more than being afraid. Collectively, people within the younger audiences would enjoy it more than older generations. From my observations, it was directed towards a younger audience by the references and cast members. This film is in no way a masterpiece but for an easy-to-watch thriller film, I’d suggest this."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I’ve avoided seeing The Dark Knight because of how much weight it carries. It’s intimidating to start something you’ve heard so much about and to leave with your own impression of it. This movie is associated with Heath Ledger and his devotion to his character known as the joker. I understand why this movie is so talked about and I’m relieved I finally watched it. In my opinion, The Dark Knight holds a place in the world of film and is worth the watch. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Before Sunrise was the finale of my film binge and leaving with a crush on Ethan Hawke. To me, this is the definition of a perfect romance movie. It’s so unrealistic but tricks you into thinking something like that could happen to you. A good romance movie is when I leave having a crush or believing I could meet a perfect man on a train to Europe who falls in love with me in the first twenty minutes of meeting."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "It left me with mixed feelings: it felt a bit empty when I thought about it later; but then I found myself thinking about it a lot the rest of the year, so something must have clicked for me. It’s a unique film."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I realize watching this film may sound like hard work to some. Though I admit it takes a fair bit of patience, I’ve always loved slower, quieter cinema, even when I was a kid (I saw Picnic at Hanging Rock when I was 9 and it fascinated me). Perhaps it works on the same part of my brain as ambient music, which is an important part of my daily listening. And diving into Jeanne Dielman was such a welcome relief compared to the boring predictability and rigid story structures of the prestige TV shows that are all we’re allowed to talk about anymore it seems."
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"Pardon me for a gushing review: this is an unreasonably great film (and now that I’m looking back, the year’s best film of any kind as far as I’m concerned). The first installment, 2019’s Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse, was great too, but the sequel expands and improves on it by a lot. It’s a masterful action film, a masterful family film — a masterful film, period.

The wildly creative, jaw-droppingly fabulous animation alone puts it in a league by itself. Honestly my brain couldn’t process how they do all this; it’s a leap forward in animation that makes me feel old and unsophisticated."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I was shocked by how bad Elemental was; and really disappointed too, considering it was one of the flagship family films of the year — a big event for my family that turned out to be a letdown. It’s allegedly a Pixar production, but the quality is more like Stan Original. Pixar is responsible for many of the greatest family films of all time, and in recent years they’e remained very consistent (Coco, Soul, and Onward are all outstanding, while Turning Red and Luca are pretty solid). I don’t think it was unrealistic to expect more from this one."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Before this my least favorite Pixar movies were Cars and The Good Dinosaur. The director of the latter, Peter Sohn, is to blame for Elemental too; but both of those ones are like Miyazaki compared to this debacle."
label: "audience_experience"

text: " if this is at all your thing, you won’t be wasting your time. It’s definitely fun and never boring, there are some memorable setpieces, and the diverse cast is nice to see (diversity in fantasy is important). Hugh Grant looking like he’s having the time of his life as the villain is arguably worth the price of rental."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I watched this one because several friends said it was far better and more entertaining than they were expecting. This had the unfortunate effect of raising my expectations too high, so that it was somewhat disappointing for me. I recommend lowering your expectations back to where they should be: a middling-to-good D&D cash-in that also serves as a satire of D&D and the fantasy genre in general, with a funny screenplay, a great cast who often phone it in, and action sequences and effects that are pretty good but not game-changing."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "One of my favorite cinema experiences of the year was rewatching Fantastic Mr. Fox with my son. It was his first time; he chose it because his class read the book in school."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I rewatched this a couple of years ago by myself, and while of course I found it brilliant — no doubt one of the best animated films ever made — it left me a bit cold. "
label: "audience_experience"

text: "This latest reboot of a tired franchise lands on the tier of unnecessary excellence — the kind of kids’ film that, while being nowhere near the level of, say, Miyazaki, or Pixar at its best, is still way more clever, imaginative and funny than it needs to be in order to make the same amount of money. If you’re not a parent I can’t explain what a pleasure and a relief that is."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "It’s more than a perfect movie because it also gives me hope."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Rolled my eyes so often and so hard I almost went blind. Said full lines of dialogue before they happened. Guessed the entire plot about ten mins in. Not half as smart, twisty, or interesting as it hopes to be, but it's so incredibly cheesy and trashy and over the top that it's ultimately a fun watch (or hate-watch)."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I hated this movie. Hated hated hated hated hated this movie. Hated it. Hated every simpering stupid vacant audience-insulting moment of it.North’ is a bad film – one of the worst movies ever made.”
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I could be inherently biased because I was a huge skateboard dork and saw it right at the height of that, but I think Lords of Dogtown is a fantastic film. Great performances from Emile Hirsch, Michael Angarano and especially Heath Ledger."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I love stardust so much! I know it was kind of a flop when it first came out, but i was expecting that it would find its audience by now..."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "Since you named The Fall which was the first movie that popped into my head, I’ll go with A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night. Really weird/cool vibe and a visually interesting movie. It’s a vampire film that ends up being an mix of Iranian culture, 50’s America, drugs, and feminist metaphors."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "The first movie is good, but it is pretty boring I can’t lie. It doesn’t really get all that interesting until the montage and the final fight. But man, something about that fight made it all worth it. I can’t describe it, just watching that iconic montage and fight made it feel like I didn’t waste my time."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "I personally loved every single one of the Harry Potter movies but there were some I enjoyed less and some I enjoyed more. In my opinion, they were all “good” movies but which in your opinion was the best and worst of the movie series? For me, the best has to be Deathly Hallows Part 1. I just felt like it was executed perfectly and lived up to my expectations of the book and it had everything expected for a build up to a finale."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "he worst movie I saw opening night in a cinema was Ultraviolet. That’s right, I went to a 11pm Thursday screening for THAT. It was fun listening to the crowd slowly turn on the movie, though."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "GET A JOB. When I was in college in like 2012/2013 I got invited to a test screening of this movie then-titled “Ready And Not” and it was the worst pile of crap I’ve ever seen. It had Bryan Cranston, Anna Kendrick, and Miles Teller. Seemed like it would be so good, but holy shit it was so bad. Some of the worst “you kids suck today!” bullshit ever and never knowing when to end a joke. When they passed around comment cards after, my whole row talked about how they gave it a zero."
label: "audience_experience"

text: "From there the movies just kept on getting better. My favorite one has to be 4. And to be honest I didn’t think 5 was that bad. It was enjoyable and had a good story, the only problem was since it was initially the last movie in the franchise, it would be a pretty bad way to end it. I’m glad they gave Rocky’s story a proper ending with Rocky Balboa (hate that name), which I also thought was a great movie. It had probably the most realistic fight in the whole franchise."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Both are Paul Newman and Robert Redford buddy movies directed by George Roy Hill that have serious things happening, but are both so breezy and fun. The score in each also helps them feel a lot lighter. Funny, entertaining, and soothing to me personally!"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Since you named The Fall which was the first movie that popped into my head, I’ll go with A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night. Really weird/cool vibe and a visually interesting movie. It’s a vampire film that ends up being an mix of Iranian culture, 50’s America, drugs, and feminist metaphors."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "The movie Sneakers is a star-studded heist movie that’s maybe 75% as cool as Ocean’s 11, which still makes it very good. Somehow, I never hear anyone talk about it."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "More importantly, I found the films incredibly lacking in any real depth. Perhaps it's because the novels are irredeemably Manichaeistic, and any attempt to imbue a modicum of nuanced discourse onto the film would be a disservice to original subject matter. Whatever the cause, I find this picture quite vapid which makes it hard to justify as being one of the "Greats.""
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I’ve said this before but I just don’t like La La Land. The music is great. The cinematography is great. The story.....is left to be desired. It left me out in the cold. I could care less about the romance between Mia and Sebastian. I could less about the characters in the film."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"Zach Synder's movie Sucker Punch is one of the worst movies I've ever seen. I know there are worse movies because there are millions of them, but as far as how high the budget was I can't think of a worse film. The acting is abysmal, the dialogue is even worse somehow, and the action looks so bad and jumbled. There is a creepy rapey vibe throughout the whole film that doesn't make any sense. The last line of the movie might be the worst ending to a film of all time.

This movie is absolutely awful and I'm generally a Zach Synder fan. If you want to gouge your eyeballs out a laugh for all the wrong reasons, I recommend checking this garbage dumpster out. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Robin Williams' bizarre 1992 film "Toys" is a masterpiece of joyful humanist filmmaking that celebrates innocence and freedom while also being disturbingly socially relevant. It predicted drone warfare, mercilessly satirizes corporate authoritarianism, and involves a number of unexpectedly moving performances from all directions. "
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Rocky I is not only the best boxing movie ever made (Yes I've seen Raging Bull), nor is it the simply the best sports movie ever made, it is also one of the top 50 movies ever made. While it's very well known and loved, I don't think most cinephiles appreciate it for how good it actually is. The soundtrack could make you believe you could run right through a brick wall and come out fine on the other side, the writing is impeccable, and some of the acting is astounding. Case in point being the scene where Mickey visits Rocky's apartment after he gets the fight against Apollo. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I've spent my entire life thinking it was Dustin Hoffman on the poster! There's some dodgier Scottish geography than 'Castle for Christmas', and it's hilarious how little Aberdeen Airport has changed in +40 years. A soft and asinine brand of boomer humour doesn't even land a chuckle. Plot-wise, a bit of a car crash with so many tangents and sub plots (CEO Abuse, skies, goths, Russians, rabbit, ceilidh). A weird and messy hodgepodge. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Looks lovely in Black and White, but I wish scenery this sexy was in full glorious colour. A bit too slick-looking, this doesn't feel like a true documentary; static camera meticulously setup for maximum visuals, and everything it records feels a little too perfect / staged."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Like a film out of time - there's nothing modern or flashy about this. You could have told me it was a low budget 80s film or 60s TV script and I'd have believed you. Small story, slightly OTT finale, couple of weird tangents, decent performances. Basic, solid, but very unremarkable."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Still, it’s good to watch with a critical eye. I hadn’t seen this film for a long time and it was really interesting to revisit. One thing that really jumped out is how wonderfully low-key it is. If it was made now, it would be adapted into a Netflix series and bloated with unnecessary subplots and CGI. It’s so refreshing how slight the narrative is, how little happens in terms of action, and how it’s mostly made up of conversations. That pacing is really what makes the emotion so impactful. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I wasn’t sure what to think after watching Jordan Peele’s third feature. I found it objectively great and fascinating, but I felt like all its elements didn’t quite gel. I was also a bit disappointed it didn’t have the impactful social commentary of Get Out (I haven’t seen his second film, Us, so I can’t compare it to that one). Of course, Peele might just want to make a weird off-kilter action-horror movie without an overt message, like Spike Lee sometimes just wants to make a detective thriller. And it does have plenty of poignant subtext, such as the erasure of Black cowboys from history, and the Black contribution to the development of cinema. "
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Written and directed by Sofia Coppola, The Virgin Suicides was unlike most of the movies I’ve seen. I was taken off guard by how it romanticizes topics that carry a lot of weight. It didn’t feel like it was bringing awareness to the subject but more of a fascination with it.  As much as this bothered me, I can’t deny that it’s an interesting story. I like Coppola because she knows how to make movies that understand the female gaze. The use of the song “Magic Man” by Heart was the perfect representation of having a crush on the “cool guy.” I’ve thought about the use of this song more times than I’d like to admit, it’s perfect. "
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "This movie covers the life of queen Marie Antoinette. It’s another film directed by Coppola and lives up to her reputation. From what I saw, there isn’t necessarily a concrete plot but it seems like that was the point. It felt more as an ode to Antoinette and her extreme lifestyle while also showing the beauty of Versailles. "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Meryl Streep is one of my favorite actors, and she plays the comical main character. The movie touches on the need for everyone to look younger while making fun of them. Even though the movie is very funny and lighthearted, there’s definitely a deeper meaning to it. I appreciate the emphasis that aging does not mean death. "
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "This movie is an edition to the Knives Out  franchise. Knives Out is by far the best, but Wake Up Dead Man is second in my opinion. It’s generally a good movie, there’s nothing about it that’s bad. The problem I have is it doesn’t have the same wow-factor as Knives Out. I do have to give a shoutout to Daniel Craig, who plays the protagonist. He makes his character extremely charming and likeable. I looked forward to watching his scenes."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "The ‘90s and 2000s rom-coms are honestly a genre in itself. Many films today try to recreate those films and yet nothing compares. Maybe it’s the actors of that time or the slightly poor quality of the camera, but films like Bridget Jones’s Diary sparks sentimental feelings. I liked how they had Hugh Grant play the a–hole. In the films I’ve seen from him, he has always been the super charismatic guy, and it was enjoyable to know he isn’t always the “perfect guy.” "
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I think the majority of people can agree this is a good movie, a group I fall into. The harsh New York accent was probably my favorite part of Moonstruck. Hearing Cher speak like that could have won her an Oscar for just the accent alone."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"Walking in to see Kit Kittredge: An American Girl, I was unaware the movie was spun off from a popular

line of toy dolls -- more than 14 million of them have been sold. Nor did I know about the anticipation and

excitement many little girls have for the movie, which is the fourth in the series (the previous three,

made for TV, are available on DVD).

I still have no opinion on the toys, but I can definitely vouch for the film living up to expectations. Shot

with the burnished, luxurious cinematography of a big-budget movie, and cast with actors who never

treat the material as if it were beneath them, Kit Kittredge: An American Girl is a thoroughly satisfying

and engaging children's picture that never forgets those kids probably didn't get to the theater by

themselves.  

"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Zoolander 2. A plot point of this movie is that Owens wilson's character has hooked up with about fifteen people in an orgy that he refers to in the same vain as his girlfriend throughout the movie and a plot is all of them got impregnated by Wilson including all of the males in the group. It then tries to take a silly, dark turn by inferring one of the dudes lost his baby due to a miscarriage, and it gets super emotional for half a second until they make a joke about it. There's a billion other reasons why this movie sucks."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Luc Besson does incredible things with visuals and cinematography. But the pacing and tone is really all over the place in a lot of his movies. This movie was so obnoxiously-badly structured, and the script wasn’t that great to begin with. The opening sequence is a blast though."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Finding Dory, which seems on the surface to be about about disability, prominently featuring Dory's memory issues counterpointed with the various issues of those at the "fish hospital". The problem is, Dory starts succeeding in the movie by just sort of conveniently remembering things at times. Side characters find more creative workarounds for their issues, as real-life disabled people must often do, but its undercut when most of Dory's issues are resolved when she can just sort of remember things sometimes. The message I guess being, don't worry too much about brain damage, it goes away."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Christmas with the Kranks. The entire town act like assholes to a couple that want to have a stress free Christmas. So you assume the town will end up being the villains. But no, it’s the opposite, the couple cave in and have the Christmas party and bow down to peer pressure and conformity."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "Back to the Future: having rich parents a fancy house and a new truck is the ultimate happy ending. Also, it's cool to keep the man who tried to rape your mom around to do chores."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
" found Finding Dory to be one of Pixar's saddest films, not so much about the manifestation of Dory's disability, but instead the loneliness that comes with it.

It's been a minute since I've seen it, but I recall Dory being abandoned often in her search. Many well-meaning characters show up and put in varying amount of effort, but when their attempts don't work perfectly they get frustrated with her and leave her to figure it out."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text:
"The themes of 'The Last Jedi', while maybe not bad within themselves - the idea that absolute good and evil don't exist, failure is a part of existence, and that chosen ones aren't real rather we all have the power to fulfill our destinies - were very weirdly and improperly placed within the context of being the 8th movie in a series of 9 films that are largely built around the complete opposite ideas. To me this makes it a weird theme, as per OP's question.

I know people are already thinking this is a "wah wah wah hate on The Last Jedi" thing, but I think this cuts to the core of why it was so divisive and why so many fans didn't like it. Yeah, the points it makes are good observations and lessons but they would've been better appreciated in a different film - a film critiquing Star Wars much in the same way that, say, 'The Boys' is a critique on the idea of superheroes as altruistic immovable beacons of goodness. As a lifelong fan of Star Wars I would have been completely fine with that and probably would've enjoyed it, much in the same way that I love both Superman and 'The Boys'. It might've even worked in a spin-off movie..."
label: "thematic_interpretation"

text: "I hate Forest Gump. I don't understand the appeal at all, the movie just drifts along without much conflict or resolution. We follow an idiot going through life, but 'succeeding' doing random things. Drama isn't created/emphasized well. Sure you have the crazy scene on the boat, but it just doesn't affect me. It's just a really boring film."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"I've never thought of a movie genre as "feel good" until now, but I suppose so. I loved "Joyeux Noël", it's the most "feel good" movie I can think of. I enjoy sentimental movies with happy themes.

If you mean the Roberto Benigni movie, then yes. I loved that movie. "
label: "audience_experience"

text:
"I didn't like Inside Out because I thought its cartoony CGI was a waste of Pixar's abilities.

More importantly, I didn't like the story. The plot (Joy and Sadness trying to get back to HQ and hitting setback after setback) was frustrating to watch and that distracted me. Seeing the various parts of Riley's brain was really fun but I think they could have found a far more interesting way to show it off than structuring the a big chunk of the plot around a series of setbacks."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I didn't enjoy 300 since the dialogue and story were flat.There was point where I was watching and instantly realized I was just watching an entire sequence probably lasted 3 minutes with no words. It completely detached me from the movie to where I actually looked around and said to myself "What am I watching?". I also saw the movie "Cars" around the same time and found the two movies to have a lot in common: Poor dialogue with a one dimensional story about building a path."
label: "craft_evaluation"

text:
"
Toy Story 3 was a perfect way to end that saga. Toy Story 4 was a shameless cash grab - although it was also a better than expected shameless cash grab.

I actually think there's a lot more story to be mined with The Incredibles but wish the sequel hadn't played it so safe.

Aging up the kids would have been the way to go in my opinion. Can you imagine the story potential of an adolescent Jack- Jack with raging hormones and all those superpowers?"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "I am as big of a Harry Potter fan as they come but I just don't think you can make an arguement for it. Its hard to compare the lasting appeal without speculating since Star Wars is much older. However, I really dont see any of the Harry Potter music having the same cultural significance that anything from Star Wars. From the epic main theme, to the ominous"
label: "craft_evaluation"

text: "Empire Strikes Back is one of the most iconic scores in history. It introduces the Imperial March, the Yoda theme and Han and Leias theme, plus it has the asteroid field sequence and sports the best closing credits suite of the entire saga. Star Wars (OT) was one of the franchises Williams was composing when he was in the process of changing pop film scores during the Spielberg/Lucas golden years and is probably his most recognizable and influential work. I think it gets the easy nod."
label: "craft_evaluation"
